steps.title: Identify what the conclusion requires
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues concluded that M. collaris associates T. caesius alarm calls specifically with danger. To support that conclusion, evidence must rule out the alternative explanation that the birds are simply startled by any unfamiliar or loud sound — it must show the reaction is particular to those alarm calls.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘In some instances, *M. collaris* froze in place or scattered into vegetation when Martinez and colleagues approached but before they began playing sounds.’: This undermines the conclusion by showing the birds react to non-alarm stimuli, confounding the results. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Other bird species than *M. collaris* also showed a tendency to freeze in place or scatter into vegetation when Martinez and colleagues played *T. caesius* alarm calls.’: This is about other species, not M. collaris; it does not support the specific conclusion about this species. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Martinez and colleagues played alarm calls from different *T. caesius* individuals and observed no significant variation in the responses of *M. collaris*.’: This shows consistency across individuals but does not prove the response is specific to danger-associated calls rather than any sound. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘When Martinez and colleagues played control sounds of random noise in the vicinity of *M. collaris*, the birds displayed no reaction.’: A control showing no reaction to random noise proves the freezing/scattering is specific to T. caesius alarm calls, directly supporting the danger-association conclusion. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
